questions.text: What is your idea of perfect happiness?
What is the trait you most deplore in yourself?
What is the trait you most deplore in others?
Which living person do you most admire?
What do you consider the most overrated virtue?
On what occasion do you lie?

answers.text: A long afternoon in the garden with nowhere to be, the light going gold, a book I've already read but love enough to reread. Happiness is never about arriving — it's about forgetting you were traveling.
Being useful. I know that sounds desperately dull, but I mean it. The feeling that something I built or fixed or said actually mattered to someone. That's the closest I get.
Sitting in a darkened cinema before the film starts. That moment of pure potential, when the story could go anywhere. I chase that feeling in everything I do.
Cooking a meal for people I love while music plays and no one checks the time. The kind of evening where you suddenly realize it's midnight and nobody wants to leave.
Running through fresh snow in a city that doesn't know me. Anonymity and beauty at the same time. Perfect happiness is always temporary — that's what makes it perfect.
Irrelevance. Not death — I've made my peace with that. But the idea of becoming background noise, of speaking and having no one turn their head.
That I've already peaked and didn't notice. That the best conversation I'll ever have, the best idea I'll ever contribute, already happened years ago on some Tuesday I can't recall.
Blindness. Not metaphorical — actual, literal blindness. I think in images. Take those away and I don't know who I am anymore.
Losing my mother's recipe book. It sounds trivial but it's the only thing of hers I have. The pages are stained with decades of oil and turmeric. It's more memoir than cookbook.
Vanity, though I dress it up as 'attention to detail.' I have spent more hours than I care to admit choosing the right pen for a letter no one will keep.
Impatience. I finish other people's sentences. I skip to the last chapter. I once left a wedding during the vows because the officiant was meandering.
Coldness. I can walk away from almost anything — relationships, cities, careers — without looking back. People mistake it for strength. It isn't.
Performative humility. The false 'Oh, it was nothing' from someone who clearly spent weeks on a task. Just own the effort.
Cruelty disguised as honesty. 'I'm just being real' is the battle cry of every bully who learned a vocabulary.
Incuriosity. Meeting someone who has no questions about the world, who never wonders why the sky is that particular blue at dusk. That absence frightens me.
My daughter. She's eleven and already braver than I've ever been. She corrected her teacher about climate data last week, with sources. I would have stayed quiet at forty-two.
My grandmother, who survived the Cultural Revolution by memorizing poetry. She says the poems kept her sane. She's ninety-three and still recites Li Bai from memory every morning.
Ursula K. Le Guin, if she were still alive. Since she isn't — my neighbor Maria, who fostered fourteen children over thirty years and never once called herself a hero.
First editions. I own a 1925 Gatsby that I've never read because I'm terrified of cracking the spine. The extravagance isn't the money — it's the absurdity of owning a book you won't open.
Travel. I'll skip meals for a month to afford a flight somewhere I've never been. My bank account is a horror show but my passport is a novel.
Loyalty. We treat it as noble but it's really just stubbornness with better PR. Loyalty to a bad idea, a bad leader, a bad relationship — that's not virtue, it's inertia.
Patience. Everyone says 'good things come to those who wait,' but in my experience, good things come to those who act while others are still contemplating the menu.
When someone shows me photos of their children. 'Oh, gorgeous!' I say, every single time, regardless of the evidence.
When I'm asked if I'm okay. The true answer is almost never appropriate for the situation.
At customs. 'Purpose of visit: tourism.' The truth — I'm running from the person I was in the last city — doesn't fit the form.

question.text: What is your idea of perfect happiness?

playerAnswer: Sitting in a darkened cinema before the film starts. That moment of pure potential, when the story could go anywhere. I chase that feeling in everything I do.
proustAnswer: My idea of happiness has changed over the years. Once it was to be surrounded by nature and the people I love. Now I think true happiness lies in the life of the mind — in reading, in reflection, in the quiet hour before sleep when the day's impressions arrange themselves into meaning.
questionLineage: This question first appeared in an English 'confession album' circa 1860, a parlor game popular among Victorian women. Young Marcel Proust answered a version of it twice — first at age 13 in 1884 ('To live near all those I love, with the beauties of nature') and again at 20 in 1892, where his answer shifted toward intellectual pleasures. The question has appeared in every major adaptation since.
